-rw-rw-r--+ 35 3646 1040 12M Apr 18 23:06 articles/2026/01/30/articles.jsonl
3731 articles/2026/01/30/articles.jsonl
- Bisan Owda platform ban, foreign leader Australia-visit threats, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Balochistan secured town, Champions League playoff draw, Crans-Montana skier crash, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 26 naming signal strong enough for a confident move.

## 2026-01-27 Update

Resolution events:
- Fury comeback opponent resolved to Arslanbek Makhmudov. The Jan. 26 Smakici story was a useful late signal but less authoritative than the earlier manager quote calling Makhmudov a top-three option. Lesson: for official opponent announcements, keep manager/promoter shortlist comments ahead of fighter self-promotion.
- Australia online-threats visit resolved to Isaac Herzog. I missed by focusing on Netanyahu/Modi. Lesson: for Australian planned-visit threat cases, include Israeli president/head-of-state visits, not only prime ministers and Indian leaders.
- Bisan Owda platform ban resolved to TikTok. I missed by overweighting Meta. Lesson: current U.S./Trump-era platform-control and censorship stories can point to TikTok even for Palestinian-journalist account bans.

Updates made:
- Australian Open men: Alcaraz beat De Minaur 7-5, 6-2, 6-1 and reached his first Melbourne semifinal; Zverev also beat Learner Tien. Removed De Minaur, raised Alcaraz, kept Sinner second ahead of his Shelton quarter, and raised Djokovic/Zverev modestly.
- Super Bowl: Jan. 27 reports said Drake Maye may have picked up a right-shoulder issue in the AFC title game, while Seattle remained the clear betting favorite. Raised Seattle to 70%.

Watched but no update:
- South Sudan displacement state, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Champions League playoff-draw questions, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Libya killing, strategic-minerals stockpile and English League Cup final had no Jan. 27 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-28 Update

Resolution events:
- South Sudan displacement state resolved to Jonglei, matching the Jan. 26 evacuation/fighting signal. Lesson: when a UN-linked South Sudan question asks for a very large displacement figure, recent state-level evacuation orders and "fiercest fighting" language can be enough to override older displacement priors.
- Netanyahu visitor resolved to Narendra Modi. I missed by staying with Trump/Rubio. Lesson: when Israel visit questions mention "soon" without a U.S.-specific clue, include major non-Western partners such as India/Modi.
- West African party dissolution resolved to Burkina Faso. I had it as a low tail behind Guinea-Bissau/Mali. Lesson: junta-governance questions about dissolving all parties should keep Burkina Faso competitive with Mali/Guinea-Bissau.

Updates made:
- Champions League draw questions: final league table put Real Madrid 9th, Inter 10th, PSG 11th, Newcastle 12th, Juventus 13th, Atletico 14th, Atalanta 15th, Leverkusen 16th, with Benfica 24th and Bodo/Glimt 23rd. Later active market text explicitly references Benfica vs Real Madrid on Feb. 17, so moved Real's playoff opponent and Benfica's first-leg opponent strongly to each other. Newcastle first leg is now Monaco or Baku from the PSG/Newcastle vs Monaco/Qarabag draw pair. Juventus's tie is against Galatasaray or Club Brugge; because the title says "eliminate Juventus", kept the upset clubs ahead of Juventus despite Juventus being seeded.
- Australian Open men: Sinner beat Shelton in straight sets; Djokovic was very lucky after Musetti retired while two sets up, and Djokovic has a toe blister. Semis are Alcaraz-Zverev and Sinner-Djokovic, with Alcaraz/Sinner close and Djokovic/Zverev as smaller live tails.
- Super Bowl: Drake Maye appeared on the projected injury report as limited with a right-shoulder issue. Raised Seattle slightly again.

Watched but no update:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Norway corruption, strategic-minerals stockpile, Libya killing and English League Cup final had no Jan. 28 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-29 Update

Resolution events:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary resolved to Howard Lutnick, matching the prior leader. Lesson: when the wording says "Trump Commerce Secretary" and the relevant correspondence is contemporary Trump-world, the current/second-term Commerce Secretary can beat the older first-term holder even if the underlying Epstein date is old.
- Crans-Montana skier crash resolved to Lindsey Vonn, matching the lead. Lesson: high-profile comeback skiers deserve meaningful mass in injury/crash markets even when the event signal is thin.
- Champions League draw cluster resolved well after the Jan. 28 table/cross-market update: Real Madrid vs Benfica, Benfica vs Real Madrid, Newcastle first leg in Baku, and Galatasaray as the club that eliminates Juventus. Lesson: active future-dated market titles can contain useful cross-market clues; the Benfica-vs-Real racist-remark question was decisive evidence for the playoff draw.

Updates made:
- Pat Cummins replacement: Jan. 1 squad reporting showed Nathan Ellis was already in Australia's provisional squad, so he is unlikely to be the official replacement. Replaced the stale Ellis tail with outside pace options led by Sean Abbott, then Spencer Johnson, Ben Dwarshuis, Jason Behrendorff and Lance Morris.
- Australian Open men: Jan. 29 previews emphasized Sinner's five-match winning streak over Djokovic and Djokovic's lucky Musetti retirement/physical concerns, while Alcaraz remains setless against Zverev. Moved Alcaraz and Sinner level at 40%, lowered Djokovic.
- Super Bowl: both Maye and Darnold appeared on injury reports, offsetting some of the previous Maye-only move. Eased Seattle from 72% to 70%.

Watched but no update:
- Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, Grammy Album of the Year, market-fire city, strategic-minerals stockpile, Norway corruption, Libya killing/forensics, English League Cup final, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 29 signal strong enough to move.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -lc "python - <<'PY'
import pandas as pd
m=pd.read_csv('market.csv', dtype={'qid':str})
print('rows',len(m),'active',(~m.is_resolved).sum(),'resolved',m.is_resolved.sum(),'no_pred_active',((~m.is_resolved)&(m.num_predictions==0)).sum())
active=m[~m.is_resolved].copy(); active['rd']=pd.to_datetime(active.resolution_date, errors='coerce')
for _,r in active.sort_values('rd').head(65).iterrows():
    print('\\n',r.qid, r.rd.date() if pd.notna(r.rd) else None, 'preds', r.num_predictions)
    print(r.title[:290])
    print('agg', r.market_aggregate)
PY"

events.preview: Pat Cummins’s replacement resolved to Ben Dwarshuis; the late correction helped but still left too much on Abbott. The next tight cluster is Jan. 31: Australian Open, Grammy, Ukraine miners bus, and Balochistan, so I’m c

blocks.text: Pat Cummins’s replacement resolved to Ben Dwarshuis; the late correction helped but still left too much on Abbott. The next tight cluster is Jan. 31: Australian Open, Grammy, Ukraine miners bus, and Balochistan, so I’m checking Jan. 30 reporting for direct results or naming clues.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -lc "python - <<'PY'
import json,textwrap
from pathlib import Path
patterns=[
 ('ao_alcaraz_zverev', ['Australian Open','Alcaraz','Zverev']),
 ('ao_sinner_djokovic', ['Australian Open','Sinner','Djokovic']),
 ('ao_final', ['Australian Open','final','Sinner']),
 ('ao_final_alcaraz', ['Australian Open','final','Alcaraz']),
 ('grammy_aoty', ['Album of the Year','Grammy']),
 ('grammy_gnx', ['GNX','Grammy']),
 ('grammy_mayhem', ['Mayhem','Grammy']),
 ('grammy_bad_bunny', ['Bad Bunny','Grammy']),
 ('miners_bus', ['bus','miners','Dnipropetrovsk']),
 ('miners_pavlohrad', ['Pavlohrad','miners']),
 ('miners_marganets', ['Marganets','miners']),
 ('balochistan_battle', ['Balochistan','three-day battle']),
 ('balochistan_secured', ['Balochistan','secured','police']),
 ('kalat_khuzdar', ['Kalat','Khuzdar']),
 ('strategic_minerals', ['strategic minerals','stockpile']),
 ('norway_corruption', ['Norwegian','prime minister','corruption']),
 ('market_fire', ['huge fire','market']),
 ('dhaka_fire', ['Dhaka','market','fire']),
 ('lagos_fire', ['Lagos','market','fire']),
 ('libya_haddad', ['Libya','Haddad','killed']),
 ('libya_forensic', ['Libya','forensic experts']),
 ('myanmar_panel', ['Myanmar','five-member panel']),
 ('league_cup_final', ['League Cup final','Manchester City']),
 ('brazil_bus_crash', ['Brazil','bus crash','16']),
 ('supreme_map', ['Supreme Court','congressional map']),
 ('ukraine_market_shelling', ['Ukraine','market shelling']),
 ('super_bowl', ['Super Bowl','Seahawks','Patriots']),
 ('gaza_troops', ['Gaza','8,000 troops']),
 ('lebanon_abducted', ['Israel','abducted','official','Lebanon']),
]
res=[]
with Path('articles/2026/01/30/articles.jsonl').open() as f:
  for i,line in enumerate(f,1):
    try:a=json.loads(line)
    except: continue
    text=(a.get('title') or '')+'\\n'+(a.get('content') or '')
    low=text.lower()
    for label,terms in patterns:
      if all(t.lower() in low for t in terms):
        pos=min([low.find(t.lower()) for t in terms if low.find(t.lower())>=0] or [0])
        snip=text[max(0,pos-300):pos+1200].replace('\\n',' ')
        res.append((label,i,a.get('date_publish'),a.get('source'),a.get('title'),snip))
        break
from collections import defaultdict
counts=defaultdict(int)
print('matches',len(res))
for label,i,date,src,title,snip in res:
  if counts[label]>=8: continue
  counts[label]+=1
  print(f\"\\n## {label} line {i} pub={date} src={src}\\nTITLE: {title}\\nSNIP: {textwrap.shorten(snip, width=1400, placeholder=' ...')}\")
print('\\ncounts')
for k in sorted(counts): print(k,counts[k])
PY"

## grammy_bad_bunny line 54 pub=2026-01-30 src=www.salon.com
TITLE: Turning Point's Super Bowl alternative to Bad Bunny looks like a flop
SNIP: Turning Point's Super Bowl alternative to Bad Bunny looks like a flop The Super Bowl is a week and a half away, but the much-ballyhooed alternative halftime show being aired by Turning Point USA has not even announced a line-up yet. To hear the public relations folks at the right-wing fundraising powerhouse founded by the late Charlie Kirk tell it, this silence is by design. Despite insisting they have “multiple performers” lined up, the TPUSA spokesperson has refused to offer any of the allegedly enticing names they promised would draw millions of viewers away from the actual Super Bowl halftime show and decimate it in the ratings. Instead, the “All-American Halftime Show” is starting to smell an awful lot like a flop. For those who have blessedly forgotten the furor from last fall, in late September the NFL announced that Bad Bunny, a Puerto Rican rapper, would headline the official program. To people whose brains haven’t been pickled by MAGA propaganda, this made sense. Bad Bunny is a true superstar, racking up streaming numbers rivaled only by Taylor Swift. The NFL is a money-making organization that wishes to build its brand with young people, so it makes sense that they want to snag the charming performer who mostly

## ao_alcaraz_zverev line 290 pub=2026-01-30 src=www.thedailybeast.com
TITLE: Tennis’ Top Rivals to Face Off in Epic Australian Open Final
SNIP: Tennis’ Top Rivals to Face Off in Epic Australian Open Final The Australian Open final is shaping up to be a grueling showdown, with top seed Carlos Alcaraz, 22, set to face rival Novak Djokovic, 38, after both won punishing five-set semifinals on Friday in Melbourne. Alcaraz advanced after outlasting Alexander Zverev in a marathon five-hour, 27-minute match in blistering heat—the longest men’s semifinal in Melbourne Park history. The Spaniard battled through apparent thigh cramps that required a medical timeout and later admitted in a on-court interview that the match was one of the most physically demanding of his career. No. 4 seed Djokovic booked his spot by defeating reigning champion Jannik Sinner in another five-set thriller, making him the oldest man in the Open era to reach the Australian Open final. Speaking on-court afterward, Djokovic said he knew beating Sinner would be “very difficult—but not impossible.” Alcaraz and Djokovic have faced off several times, most recently at the 2025 U.S. Open, where Alcaraz stunned Djokovic for the title.

## ao_alcaraz_zverev line 456 pub=2026-01-30 src=www.khaleejtimes.com
TITLE: Keep writing me off, says Djokovic winning Australian Open thriller
SNIP: Keep writing me off, says Djokovic winning Australian Open thriller Novak Djokovic called it one of his best performances in a decade after outlasting Jannik Sinner early Saturday in a five-set marathon to set up an Australian Open final against Carlos Alcaraz. The 38-year-old Serb rolled back the years to battle past two-time reigning champion Sinner 3-6, 6-3, 4-6, 6-4, 6-4 in a 1:32 am finish. Djokovic plays top seed Alcaraz in Sunday's Melbourne title decider and if he beats a player 16 years younger, he would win an all-time record 25th Grand Slam crown. After four hours and nine minutes of nail-biting action, Djokovic kneeled to the court floor. He appeared emotional, lost for words at first. "It feels surreal, to be honest," said Djokovic, who had lost the five previous matches to the 24-year-old Italian Sinner. He added: "I have tremendous respect for him, an incredible player. He pushes you to the very limit, which is what he did tonight to me." With age and injuries catching up with Djokovic, Sunday may represent the Serb's best chance of seizing that elusive 25th major, although his gritty display against Sinner shows he still has plenty left in the tank. His last Grand Slam title came at the US Open in 2023, sinc

## ao_alcaraz_zverev line 495 pub=2026-01-30 src=www.irishtimes.com
TITLE: Carlos Alcaraz beats Alexander Zverev in Australian Open epic lasting over five hours
SNIP: Carlos Alcaraz beats Alexander Zverev in Australian Open epic lasting over five hours Carlos Alcaraz has beaten Alexander Zverev in a five-set thriller in the Australian Open semi-final, defeating the German 6-4, 7-6 (7-5), 6-7 (3-7), 6-7 (4-7), 7-5. The world number one Alcaraz had looked set for victory after winning the first two sets but struggled for mobility in the third set, leading to a medical timeout. Zverev was furious at Alcaraz taking the timeout for what he thought was cramp, saying him and Sinner got “protected”. “It’s unbelievable that he gets treated for cramps, it’s BS. You are protecting these two guys all the time,” he said to the umpire. The perceived injustice inspired Zverev to some of his best tennis, winning the third and fourth sets, and serving for the match in the fifth set. But the remarkable Alcaraz broke Zverev and won the final set tiebreaker in an epic lasting 5 hours and 27 minutes. Alcaraz will play the winner of 10-time Australian Open winner Novak Djokovic and rival Jannik Sinner in the final. Winning the Australian Open would complete the career grand slam. More to follow ...

## ao_alcaraz_zverev line 551 pub=2026-01-30 src=www.irishtimes.com
TITLE: Novak Djokovic rolls back the years to beat Jannik Sinner in Australian Open epic
SNIP: Novak Djokovic rolls back the years to beat Jannik Sinner in Australian Open epic Novak Djokovic has beaten Jannik Sinner in the Australian Open semi-final after another five-set epic on a remarkable day of tennis in Melbourne. The 10-time champion beat Sinner, who had won the previous two Australian Opens, 3-6, 6-3, 4-6, 6-4, 6-4 to reach yet another Grand Slam final. The 38-year-old rolled back the years with a superb performance to set up a final with Carlos Alcaraz, who beat Alexander Zverev in five sets earlier. The Serb is looking for a record 25th Grand Slam. More to follow... Earlier... the Alcaraz-Zverev report Murmurs around Melbourne Park had been building. That the men’s Australian Open draw had not met expectations in 2026. That matches had been one-sided, and lacking memorable moments. That so-called SinCaraz was a foregone conclusion. That tennis had lost its touch. Murmur no more. In this year’s first match on Rod Laver Arena to go five sets, Carlos Alcaraz leapt off the canvas to outlast Alexander Zverev 6-4, 7-6 (5), 6-7 (3), 6-7 (4), 7-5 over five hours and 27 minutes – the third longest match in Australian Open history. It was so long it pushed the second men’s semi-final back more than an hour and left thousands watching

## ao_sinner_djokovic line 601 pub=2026-01-28 src=www.straitstimes.com
TITLE: Novak Djokovic through to Australian Open semi-finals after Lorenzo Musetti injury
SNIP: Novak Djokovic through to Australian Open semi-finals after Lorenzo Musetti injury MELBOURNE – Novak Djokovic's quest for a record Grand Slam title continued in dramatic circumstances after fifth seed Lorenzo Musetti quit their Australian Open quarter-final match with an injury after taking a two-set lead at Melbourne Park on Jan 28. Musetti's retirement when leading 6-4, 6-3, 1-3 meant Djokovic pulled off a great escape at his most successful hunting ground, with the 10-times champion's bid for a standalone 25th Major to break the tie with Margaret Court still alive. "I don't know what to say, except that I feel really sorry for him and he was a far better player," Djokovic said. "I was on my way home tonight. These things happen in sport and it's happened to me a few times, but being in quarter-finals of a Grand Slam, two sets to love up and being in full control, I mean it's so unfortunate. "I wish him a speedy recovery and he should have been the winner today, there's no doubt." The 38-year-old Serb returned to action after an extended break following fourth-round opponent Jakub Mensik's walkover on Jan 25, and his freshness showed during a sharp start where he brought plenty of vari

SNIP: HIGHLIGHTS-Tennis-Australian Open day 10 Jan 27 - Highlights of the 10th day at the Australian Open on Tuesday (times GMT): 0905 SVITOLINA CRUSHES GAUFF IN 59 MINUTES Elina Svitolina upset Coco Gauff 6-1 6-2 to move into her first Australian Open semi-final, where she will meet top seed Aryna Sabalenka. TITLE: Alcaraz shines, Sabalenka beats the heat to make Australian Open semi-finals
SNIP: Alcaraz shines, Sabalenka beats the heat to make Australian Open semi-finals MELBOURNE, Jan 27 - Carlos Alcaraz sizzled under the bright lights at the Australian Open and blazed into the semi-finals on Tuesday after fellow top seed Aryna Sabalenka remained cool in the unforgiving Melbourne heat to stay on course for another title. Alexander Zverev was briefly made to sweat under the roof of Rod Laver Arena before overcoming Learner Tien, but a frustrated Coco Gauff unravelled in a racket-smashing meltdown following a stunning defeat by the resurgent Elina Svitolina. That preceded Alcaraz's dominant 7-5 6-2 6-1 victory over Alex de Minaur on the main showcourt, as the Spaniard dashed his sixth-seeded opponent's hopes of ending a five-decade Australian wait for a homegrown men's champion. The 22-year-old -- a six-times major winner -- is seeking his own piece of history at Melbourne Park, where he can eclipse Don Budge and become the youngest man to lift all four Grand Slam trophies. "It was a great match in terms of level, in terms of the intensity. I played the best match so far in the tournament," Alcaraz told reporters. "This is my first official tournament of the year and someti

## ao_alcaraz_zverev line 791 pub=2026-01-27 src=www.straitstimes.com
TITLE: Carlos Alcaraz crushes Alex De Minaur to reach Australian Open semi-finals
SNIP: Carlos Alcaraz crushes Alex De Minaur to reach Australian Open semi-finals – Carlos Alcaraz swept into the Australian Open semi-finals for the first time on Jan 27 and a clash with Alexander Zverev to take a step closer to tennis history. The Spanish world No. 1 silenced a partisan Melbourne crowd in brushing aside outclassed home hope Alex de Minaur 7-5, 6-2, 6-1 at Rod Laver Arena. - South Sudan displacement state, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Champions League playoff-draw questions, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Libya killing, strategic-minerals stockpile and English League Cup final had no Jan. 27 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-28 Update

Resolution events:
- South Sudan displacement state resolved to Jonglei, matching the Jan. 26 evacuation/fighting signal. Lesson: when a UN-linked South Sudan question asks for a very large displacement figure, recent state-level evacuation orders and "fiercest fighting" language can be enough to override older displacement priors.
- Netanyahu visitor resolved to Narendra Modi. I missed by staying with Trump/Rubio. Lesson: when Israel visit questions mention "soon" without a U.S.-specific clue, include major non-Western partners such as India/Modi.
- West African party dissolution resolved to Burkina Faso. I had it as a low tail behind Guinea-Bissau/Mali. Lesson: junta-governance questions about dissolving all parties should keep Burkina Faso competitive with Mali/Guinea-Bissau.

Updates made:
- Champions League draw questions: final league table put Real Madrid 9th, Inter 10th, PSG 11th, Newcastle 12th, Juventus 13th, Atletico 14th, Atalanta 15th, Leverkusen 16th, with Benfica 24th and Bodo/Glimt 23rd. Later active market text explicitly references Benfica vs Real Madrid on Feb. 17, so moved Real's playoff opponent and Benfica's first-leg opponent strongly to each other. Newcastle first leg is now Monaco or Baku from the PSG/Newcastle vs Monaco/Qarabag draw pair. Juventus's tie is against Galatasaray or Club Brugge; because the title says "eliminate Juventus", kept the upset clubs ahead of Juventus despite Juventus being seeded.
- Australian Open men: Sinner beat Shelton in straight sets; Djokovic was very lucky after Musetti retired while two sets up, and Djokovic has a toe blister. Semis are Alcaraz-Zverev and Sinner-Djokovic, with Alcaraz/Sinner close and Djokovic/Zverev as smaller live tails.
- Super Bowl: Drake Maye appeared on the projected injury report as limited with a right-shoulder issue. Raised Seattle slightly again.

Watched but no update:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Norway corruption, strategic-minerals stockpile, Libya killing and English League Cup final had no Jan. 28 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-29 Update

Resolution events:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary resolved to Howard Lutnick, matching the prior leader. Lesson: when the wording says "Trump Commerce Secretary" and the relevant correspondence is contemporary Trump-world, the current/second-term Commerce Secretary can beat the older first-term holder even if the underlying Epstein date is old.
- Crans-Montana skier crash resolved to Lindsey Vonn, matching the lead. Lesson: high-profile comeback skiers deserve meaningful mass in injury/crash markets even when the event signal is thin.
- Champions League draw cluster resolved well after the Jan. 28 table/cross-market update: Real Madrid vs Benfica, Benfica vs Real Madrid, Newcastle first leg in Baku, and Galatasaray as the club that eliminates Juventus. Lesson: active future-dated market titles can contain useful cross-market clues; the Benfica-vs-Real racist-remark question was decisive evidence for the playoff draw.

Updates made:
- Pat Cummins replacement: Jan. 1 squad reporting showed Nathan Ellis was already in Australia's provisional squad, so he is unlikely to be the official replacement. Replaced the stale Ellis tail with outside pace options led by Sean Abbott, then Spencer Johnson, Ben Dwarshuis, Jason Behrendorff and Lance Morris.
- Australian Open men: Jan. 29 previews emphasized Sinner's five-match winning streak over Djokovic and Djokovic's lucky Musetti retirement/physical concerns, while Alcaraz remains setless against Zverev. Moved Alcaraz and Sinner level at 40%, lowered Djokovic.
- Super Bowl: both Maye and Darnold appeared on injury reports, offsetting some of the previous Maye-only move. Eased Seattle from 72% to 70%.

Watched but no update:
- Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, Grammy Album of the Year, market-fire city, strategic-minerals stockpile, Norway corruption, Libya killing/forensics, English League Cup final, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 29 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-30 Update

Resolution events:
- Pat Cummins replacement resolved to Ben Dwarshuis. The Jan. 29 correction helped by adding him, but I still overweighted Sean Abbott. Lesson: for Australia squad injury replacements in T20 conditions, left-arm domestic/franchise pace options such as Ben Dwarshuis and Spencer Johnson need more mass, especially when the obvious named seamers are already in the squad.

Updates made:
- Australian Open men: both semifinals went five sets. Alcaraz beat Zverev after a 5h27 match with cramp/medical-timeout concerns; Djokovic beat Sinner in five and reached the final. Moved the market to Alcaraz 60%, Djokovic 40%.
- Grammy Album of the Year: a Jan. 30 Independent preview tipped Bad Bunny's "Debi Tirar Mas Fotos" for Album of the Year, adding to earlier Lady Gaga/Kendrick arguments. Raised Bad Bunny to level with Kendrick and close behind Gaga.
- Super Bowl: Drake Maye missed Friday practice with a right-shoulder issue plus illness, though the Patriots said availability was not a major concern. Raised Seattle to 73%.
